metadata.description: Luganda sentence pairs for greetings, introductions, farewells, and polite conversation
metadata.verification_notes: Corrections made: gr_020 had a fused sentence error — Luganda contained two separate sentences merged into one entry. Corrected and separated. gr_022 and gr_023 corrected from stored infinitives to actual spoken greeting forms. Notes expanded across all entries for AI context quality.

entries.notes: Koodi = greeting used when arriving or knocking at a door. Kalibu = welcome (widely used, borrowed term).

entries.english: We are happy to see you.
entries.notes: Tusanyuse = we are happy. Okukulaba = to see you.

entries.english: I am happy too. / Me too.
entries.notes: Nange = me too / I also. Nsanyuse = I am happy.
Eradde = is it calm / is it fine. Ssebo = polite address for a man (Sir).
Muliyo = are you there. Mutyanno = how are you (plural). Ssebo = Sir.

entries.english: Thank you for all you are doing.
entries.notes: Mugyebale = thank you (plural/formal). Emirimu = the work / the tasks.

entries.english: How is the rest of the family?
entries.notes: Abeeka = the others at home. Obalese = you left them. Batya = how are they.
Eka = at home. Eradde = is it all fine / peaceful.

entries.english: We are okay, Madam. We work just for a living, and at home people are fine.
entries.luganda: Gyetuli bulungi nnyabo. Tukola tubeewo, n'abeeka balungi.
entries.notes: Nnyabo = polite address for a woman (Madam). Tukola tubeewo = we work to get by. Abeeka balungi = the others at home are well.

entries.english: It has been a long time since we last saw you.
entries.notes: Tuludde = we have been waiting long. Okubalaba = to see you all.

entries.english: Even us — the world is so busy that we cannot spare time to see each other.
entries.luganda: Naffe; anti olaba ensi etumazeeko ekiseera okulabagana ate n'emitawaana mingi.
entries.notes: Naffe = even us. Ensi etumazeeko = the world has consumed our time. Emitawaana = obstacles / competing demands.

entries.english: Congratulations on building. It is a big house.
entries.notes: Mwebale = well done / thank you (plural). Kuzimba = for building. Ennyumba nene = a big house.
Nate = also / in addition / as well. Used to add to a previous statement.
Mangu = quickly / soon. Nga bwe kisoboka = as it is possible.

entries.english: Please help me and tell me.
entries.notes: Nyamba = help me. Era = and. Ombuulire = tell me / inform me.

entries.english: Excuse me — do you understand English?
entries.notes: Mwattu nsonyiwa = excuse me / I am sorry. Omanyi = do you know. Oluzungu = the English language.

entries.english: Have pity on me, it is very bad.
entries.notes: Nsaasira = have mercy / pity me. Kibi nnyo ddala = it is very bad indeed.

entries.english: I am disappointed, I am in a hurry, and I am still searching.
entries.luganda: Nsaaliddwa, ndi mu bwangu era noonya.
entries.notes: Nsaaliddwa = I am let down / disappointed. Ndi mu bwangu = I am in a hurry. Noonya = I am searching / looking.

entries.english: I think there is cold and warmth today.
entries.notes: Ndowooza = I think / I believe. Empewo = cold / wind. Ebbugumu = warmth / heat.

entries.english: It is my fault. I do not know if it is true.
entries.notes: Nsobi yange = it is my mistake / my fault. Simanyi = I do not know. Oba kituufu = whether it is true. CORRECTED: previous version incorrectly merged this entry with gr_021.

entries.english: Wait a moment, here is the direction.
entries.notes: Lindako katono = wait a little moment. Endagiriro yiino = here is the direction / the pointer.
Weeraba = goodbye (said to the person staying). Nsiibuula = I am taking my leave. CORRECTED: previously stored as the infinitive 'Okusiibuula' which is not the used spoken form.
Kalibu = welcome (widely used form). Tukwanirizza = we welcome you (native Luganda form). CORRECTED: previously stored as infinitive 'Okwaniriza' which is not the spoken greeting.
Ova wa = where do you come from. The longer form 'Gwe ova wa?' is also correct but the short form is more natural in speech.
Ogenda = you are going. Wa = where.

entries.english: Which way will you pass through?
entries.notes: Onoyita = you will pass through. Wa = where / which way.
Okoma = you reach / stop at a destination. Wa = where.
Okomawo = you come back here. Ddi = when.
Lwaki = why. Ogenda = you are going.

entries.english: Will you pass by the market?
entries.notes: Onoyitako = will you pass by there. Mu katale = at/through the market.

entries.english: Which route will you return by?
entries.notes: Onoddira = you will return. Mu kkubo ki = by which path / route.
Azze = he/she has come / arrived. Used when someone has just appeared.
Addayo = he/she goes back / returns to where they came from.
Akomawo = he/she will return here (to this place).
Adduse = he/she has run (completed). Implies running away or fleeing.
Ayimiridde = he/she is standing (present state).
Atudde = he/she is sitting / seated (present state).
Atambula = he/she walks / is walking.
Kampitewo = let me pass / allow me through. Used when asking for space in a crowd.
Kankulinde = let me wait for you. A polite offer to wait.

entries.english: Try it. / Give it a go.
entries.notes: Gezaako = try it / have a go at it. Used to encourage someone to attempt something.
Sembera = come nearer / approach. Used to beckon someone closer.
Toninyako = do not tread on me. To- prefix marks the negative command.
Nkwata = hold me / take me. Mu ngalo = in the palm / hand.
Weeyongereyo = extend yourself forward / move up. Used in a crowded bus or queue.
Bannange = my friends / dear ones. Muli mutya = how are you (plural).
Webale = thank you (singular). Mwebale = thank you (plural/formal). Nnyo = very much.

entries.english: Come back another time. / You are always welcome.
entries.notes: Mukomangawo = come back here (plural). Olulala = another time / one day. Said when a guest is leaving.
